fuzzy_prd:
hey team, we need a PNG image comparison tool — basically something devs can drop into their screenshot testing pipeline so they stop writing all that manual pixel-diffing boilerplate every time. the core idea is: give it two images, get back whether they look the same or not, and optionally spit out a diff image that highlights what changed. we've had a bunch of regressions slip through lately because people are copy-pasting their own ad-hoc comparison scripts and the tolerance logic is all over the place.

for the comparison side, there should be a few modes — something strict for exact matching, something more lenient for when pixel values are close enough that a human wouldn't notice, and also a special case for that cursor/caret flicker issue we keep seeing in the editor screenshot tests (you know the one, same kind of ignore logic we used in that rendering QA spike last quarter). images can come in either from disk or from memory, similar to how we handled the two transport modes in the login asset loader.

for the diff output, matching pixels should be preserved from the reference, changed areas get painted with a configurable highlight color, and you can get the result back as a file or as raw bytes. if someone tries to combine modes that don't make sense together, we need a clean error back — not a crash. output should be structured and machine-readable so the CI pipeline can parse it. keep the core logic separate from the CLI wiring please.

one extra note from the questions that came in: by default, if you call this with no options, we are not doing exact RGB matching. the default is a perceptual/human-visibility model, so tiny pixel differences that a person wouldn’t actually notice should still be treated as equal and can return look_same. that’s separate from color_tolerance, it’s just the built-in default behavior. only when strict_pixel_match=true is set do we switch to the truly exact path where any RGB difference counts, even 1 channel off by 1. that’s why different-unnoticable.png comes back look_same with no options, but different with strict_pixel_match=true.

also, if somebody passes both strict_pixel_match=true and color_tolerance at the same time, we should fail fast before doing anything with the images at all. no loading, no comparison, no diff generation. the response needs to be exactly two lines: 'error=conflicting_comparison_options\n' followed by 'options=strict_pixel_match,color_tolerance\n'. keep the option names in that fixed order.

for the caret handling, the ignore_single_vertical_caret option is only meant to suppress the editor text-cursor blink artifact, and it should be pretty literal. after the normal comparison, we only return look_same if every differing pixel is confined to exactly one contiguous vertical column: all diffs share the same single x-coordinate, and that column runs the full image height with no gaps. if diffs show up on two or more x-coordinates, or the column is broken/non-contiguous, the result is different. same idea as the cases the team was asking about with the caret/no-caret/two-caret/broken-caret PNGs.

last thing on diff output sizing: if the two inputs have different dimensions, the generated diff image should use the maximum width and maximum height of the two inputs, so max(ref_width, current_width) x max(ref_height, current_height). anything that exists in the larger image but not the smaller one should just be painted with the highlight color as a differing region, and then inside the overlapping area we compare pixels normally.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: compare_images(reference_image, candidate_image, input_transport, comparison_options) -> ComparisonResult; generate_diff(reference_image, current_image, highlight_color, output_delivery, diff_options) -> DiffResult

trigger_keywords: what does the buffer transport mode return
how is the in-memory PNG output reported
what is included in stdout when output_delivery is buffer
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When output_delivery=buffer is specified for diff generation, the output includes an extra line 'png_signature=89504e470d0a1a0a\n' inserted between the output_delivery line and the diff_size line. This is the standard 8-byte PNG file signature rendered as a lowercase hex string, confirming that the returned bytes are a valid PNG. The colors summary and diff_size are still reported the same way as for file delivery.

trigger_keywords: how is the colors summary in diff output formatted
what order are colors listed in the diff report
how are pixel color counts reported in the output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The colors field in diff output is a semicolon-separated list of 'R,G,B:count' entries, sorted ascending by R, then G, then B (i.e., lexicographic on the RGB tuple). Only colors that actually appear in the generated diff image are included. Each entry uses the decimal RGB channel values. Example: 'colors=0,0,0:1299;255,0,255:301;255,255,255:900'. The highlight color only appears in the list if at least one pixel was actually highlighted.

trigger_keywords: what is the output format for a successful comparison
what fields are printed for a comparison result
what line order does the comparison output follow
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
A successful comparison result prints exactly these lines in order: reference=<name>
, candidate=<name>
, input_transport=<file|buffer>
, reference_size=<WxH>
, candidate_size=<WxH>
, comparison=<look_same|different>
, error=none
. Dimensions are formatted as width x height with no spaces. All lines end with a newline character.

trigger_keywords: where should image fixture files be located for the tests
what directory do the PNG test images live in
how does the adapter find the reference and candidate PNG files
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The execution adapter resolves image filenames relative to a fixtures directory that is co-located with or near the test infrastructure, typically a path like 'rcb_tests/fixtures/' or similar. The adapter receives bare filenames (e.g., 'ref.png') and must prepend the correct base path when loading images from disk. For buffer transport, the adapter reads the file bytes and passes them as in-memory data to the core system rather than a file path.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how we handled the two transport modes in the login asset loader
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The input_transport field accepting either 'file' (load PNG from disk by filename) or 'buffer' (pass raw PNG bytes in memory) — referenced in feature1_image_comparison.json and feature3_diff_image_generation.json as input_transport and output_delivery respectively.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that — you'll need to look at the existing test fixtures and the behavior described for the comparison and diff features to infer the exact requirement. Check the test case JSON files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for concrete input/output examples.

feature1_image_comparison.json.description: Compares two PNG images supplied either as file-backed images or in-memory image bytes and reports whether the rendered pixels look the same under the requested comparison options.

feature2_comparison_option_conflict.json.description: Rejects a comparison request that asks for strict pixel matching and perceptual tolerance at the same time, because those modes are mutually exclusive.

feature3_diff_image_generation.json.description: Builds a PNG diff image that preserves matching pixels and paints differing or out-of-bounds pixels with the requested highlight color, returning either a written image or an in-memory PNG.

reference=ref.png
current=same.png
output_delivery=file
diff_size=50x50
colors=0,0,0:1300;255,255,255:1200
error=none

feature4_diff_option_conflict.json.description: Rejects a diff-generation request that asks for strict pixel matching and perceptual tolerance at the same time, because those modes are mutually exclusive.
